content.text: In Chinese, '你好' means {{c1::Hello / Hi}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐ hǎo}}.
content.explanation: '你好' (Nǐ hǎo) is the standard, universal greeting in Mandarin Chinese. It literally means 'You good?' and is used at any time of day.
content.spanish: En chino, '你好' significa 'Hola'. Pronunciación: Nǐ hǎo.
content.usage:
Term: 你好 (Nǐ hǎo) | Use: General greeting for anyone
  • 你好! 我叫 Jesus。 — Hello! My name is Jesus.

content.text: In Chinese, '早上好' means {{c1::Good morning}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Zǎo shang hǎo}}.
content.explanation: '早上好' (Zǎo shang hǎo) is a morning greeting used before noon. '早' (zǎo) alone is a casual shortcut, like saying 'Morning!'
content.spanish: En chino, '早上好' significa 'Buenos días'. Pronunciación: Zǎo shang hǎo.
content.usage:
Term: 早上好 (Zǎo shang hǎo) | Use: Morning greeting
  • 早上好! 今天天气真好。 — Good morning! The weather is beautiful today.

content.text: In Chinese, '晚上好' means {{c1::Good evening}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǎnshang hǎo}}.
content.explanation: '晚上好' (Wǎnshang hǎo) is used in the evening as a polite greeting. '晚' means 'late/evening'.
content.spanish: En chino, '晚上好' significa 'Buenas noches'. Pronunciación: Wǎnshang hǎo.
content.usage:
Term: 晚上好 (Wǎnshang hǎo) | Use: Evening greeting
  • 晚上好! 欢迎来到这里。 — Good evening! Welcome here.

content.text: In Chinese, '再见' means {{c1::Goodbye}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Zàijiàn}}.
content.explanation: '再见' (Zàijiàn) is the standard farewell. It literally means 'See again' (再=again, 见=see), similar to the French 'au revoir'.
content.spanish: En chino, '再见' significa 'Adiós'. Pronunciación: Zàijiàn.
content.usage:
Term: 再见 (Zàijiàn) | Use: Standard farewell
  • 再见! 明天见。 — Goodbye! See you tomorrow.

content.scenario: Chinese A1: Nice to Meet You 🗣️
content.text: In Chinese, '很高兴认识你' means {{c1::Nice to meet you}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Hěn gāoxìng rènshí nǐ}}.
content.explanation: '很高兴认识你' is the standard phrase when meeting someone for the first time. It literally means 'Very happy to know you'.
content.spanish: En chino, '很高兴认识你' significa 'Mucho gusto'. Pronunciación: Hěn gāoxìng rènshí nǐ.
content.usage:
Term: 很高兴认识你 | Use: First meeting
  • 很高兴认识你! 你是哪里人? — Nice to meet you! Where are you from?

content.text: In Chinese, '你好吗?' means {{c1::How are you?}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐ hǎo ma?}}.
content.explanation: '你好吗?' adds the question particle '吗' (ma) to the greeting '你好'. A common response is '我很好' (Wǒ hěn hǎo) — I'm very well.
content.spanish: En chino, '你好吗?' significa '¿Cómo estás?'. Pronunciación: Nǐ hǎo ma?
content.usage:
Term: 你好吗? (Nǐ hǎo ma?)
  • A: 你好吗? — B: 我很好，谢谢! (I'm very well, thank you!)

content.text: In Chinese, '谢谢' means {{c1::Thank you}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Xièxie}}.
content.explanation: '谢谢' (Xièxie) is the most common way to express gratitude. A polite response is '不客气' (Bú kèqi) — You're welcome.
content.spanish: En chino, '谢谢' significa 'Gracias'. Pronunciación: Xièxie.
content.usage:
Term: 谢谢 (Xièxie)
  • A: 谢谢! — B: 不客气! (You're welcome!)

content.text: In Chinese, '不客气' means {{c1::You're welcome}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Bú kèqi}}.
content.explanation: '不客气' (Bú kèqi) literally means 'don't be polite'. It's the standard response to '谢谢'. Another common alternative is '没事' (Méi shì) — No problem.
content.spanish: En chino, '不客气' significa 'De nada'. Pronunciación: Bú kèqi.
content.usage:
Term: 不客气 (Bú kèqi)
  • A: 谢谢你的帮助! — B: 不客气!

content.text: In Chinese, '对不起' means {{c1::Sorry / I apologize}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Duìbuqǐ}}.
content.explanation: '对不起' (Duìbuqǐ) is used for sincere apologies. For a lighter 'excuse me' to get someone's attention, use '不好意思' (Bù hǎoyìsi).
content.spanish: En chino, '对不起' significa 'Lo siento'. Pronunciación: Duìbuqǐ.
content.usage:
Term: 对不起 (Duìbuqǐ)
  • 对不起，我来晚了。 — Sorry, I'm late.

content.text: In Chinese, '明天见' means {{c1::See you tomorrow}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Míngtiān jiàn}}.
content.explanation: '明天见' combines '明天' (míngtiān = tomorrow) and '见' (jiàn = see). This structure is flexible: '下周见' = see you next week.
content.spanish: En chino, '明天见' significa 'Hasta mañana'. Pronunciación: Míngtiān jiàn.
content.usage:
Term: 明天见 (Míngtiān jiàn)
  • 好的，明天见! — OK, see you tomorrow!

content.text: In Chinese, '晚安' means {{c1::Good night}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǎn'ān}}.
content.explanation: '晚安' (Wǎn'ān) is used as a farewell at night or before bed. '晚' = evening/late, '安' = peace/safe.
content.spanish: En chino, '晚安' significa 'Buenas noches (al despedirse)'. Pronunciación: Wǎn'ān.
content.usage:
Term: 晚安 (Wǎn'ān)
  • 晚安，好梦! — Good night, sweet dreams!

content.text: In Chinese, '欢迎' means {{c1::Welcome}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Huānyíng}}.
content.explanation: '欢迎' (Huānyíng) is used to welcome guests or visitors. Shops and restaurants often display '欢迎光临' (Huānyíng guānglín) — Welcome (honored guest).
content.spanish: En chino, '欢迎' significa 'Bienvenido'. Pronunciación: Huānyíng.
content.usage:
Term: 欢迎 (Huānyíng)
  • 欢迎来到北京! — Welcome to Beijing!

content.scenario: Chinese A1: Long Time No See 🗣️
content.text: In Chinese, '好久不见' means {{c1::Long time no see}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Hǎo jiǔ bù jiàn}}.
content.explanation: Interestingly, the English phrase 'long time no see' is believed to be a direct calque from the Chinese '好久不见'. It is widely used in casual reunions.
content.spanish: En chino, '好久不见' significa '¡Cuánto tiempo sin verte!'. Pronunciación: Hǎo jiǔ bù jiàn.
content.usage:
Term: 好久不见 (Hǎo jiǔ bù jiàn)
  • 好久不见！你最近怎么样? — Long time no see! How have you been lately?

content.text: In Chinese, '请' means {{c1::Please}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Qǐng}}.
content.explanation: '请' (Qǐng) can mean 'please', 'invite', or 'treat (to a meal)'. As a polite particle it's placed at the start of requests: '请坐' = Please sit down.
content.spanish: En chino, '请' significa 'Por favor'. Pronunciación: Qǐng.
content.usage:
Term: 请 (Qǐng)
  • 请跟我来。 — Please follow me.
  • 请问，洗手间在哪里? — Excuse me, where is the restroom?

content.scenario: Chinese A1: What is Your Name 🗣️
content.text: In Chinese, '你叫什么名字?' means {{c1::What is your name?}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐ jiào shénme míngzi?}}
content.explanation: '你叫什么名字?' — literally 'You are called what name?' — is the standard way to ask someone's name. The answer is '我叫…' (Wǒ jiào…) — My name is…
content.spanish: En chino, '你叫什么名字?' significa '¿Cómo te llamas?'. Pronunciación: Nǐ jiào shénme míngzi?
content.usage:
Term: 你叫什么名字?
  • A: 你叫什么名字? — B: 我叫 Jesus。(My name is Jesus.)

content.scenario: Chinese A1: Where Are You From 🗣️
content.text: In Chinese, '你是哪里人?' means {{c1::Where are you from?}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐ shì nǎlǐ rén?}}
content.explanation: '你是哪里人?' literally means 'You are where person?' The answer is '我是墨西哥人' (Wǒ shì Mòxīgē rén) — I am Mexican.
content.spanish: En chino, '你是哪里人?' significa '¿De dónde eres?'. Pronunciación: Nǐ shì nǎlǐ rén?
content.usage:
Term: 你是哪里人?
  • A: 你是哪里人? — B: 我是墨西哥人。(I'm Mexican.)

content.text: In Chinese, '我听不懂' means {{c1::I don't understand (listening)}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǒ tīng bu dǒng}}.
content.explanation: '我听不懂' specifically means 'I cannot understand what I heard'. For not understanding written text, use '我看不懂'. A useful learner phrase.
content.spanish: En chino, '我听不懂' significa 'No entiendo (lo que escucho)'. Pronunciación: Wǒ tīng bu dǒng.
content.usage:
Term: 我听不懂 (Wǒ tīng bu dǒng)
  • 对不起，我听不懂。请说慢一点。 — Sorry, I don't understand. Please speak more slowly.

content.scenario: Chinese A1: Can You Speak Slower 🗣️
content.text: In Chinese, '请说慢一点' means {{c1::Please speak more slowly}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Qǐng shuō màn yīdiǎn}}.
content.explanation: An essential phrase for language learners. '慢' (màn) = slow, '一点' (yīdiǎn) = a little. Together: 'a little slower'.
content.spanish: En chino, '请说慢一点' significa 'Por favor habla más despacio'. Pronunciación: Qǐng shuō màn yīdiǎn.
content.usage:
Term: 请说慢一点
  • 你能说慢一点吗? — Can you speak a little slower?

content.scenario: Chinese A1: Do You Speak English 🗣️
content.text: In Chinese, '你会说英语吗?' means {{c1::Do you speak English?}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐ huì shuō Yīngyǔ ma?}}
content.explanation: '会说' (huì shuō) means 'can speak'. '英语' is English. To ask about Spanish: '你会说西班牙语吗?' (Nǐ huì shuō Xībānyáyǔ ma?)
content.spanish: En chino, '你会说英语吗?' significa '¿Hablas inglés?'. Pronunciación: Nǐ huì shuō Yīngyǔ ma?
content.usage:
Term: 你会说英语吗?
  • A: 你会说英语吗? — B: 会一点。(A little.)

content.text: In Chinese, '我很好' means {{c1::I'm very well / I'm fine}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǒ hěn hǎo}}.
content.explanation: '我很好' is the standard positive response to '你好吗?'. '很' (hěn) intensifies the adjective — it is almost always required in such sentences.
content.spanish: En chino, '我很好' significa 'Estoy muy bien'. Pronunciación: Wǒ hěn hǎo.
content.usage:
Term: 我很好 (Wǒ hěn hǎo)
  • A: 你好吗? — B: 我很好，谢谢! 你呢? (I'm well, thank you! And you?)

content.text: In Chinese, '我' means {{c1::I / me}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǒ}}.
content.explanation: '我' (Wǒ) is the first-person singular pronoun in Chinese. Unlike English, Chinese uses the same word for both subject ('I') and object ('me').
content.spanish: En chino, '我' significa 'Yo / Me'. Pronunciación: Wǒ.
content.usage:
Term: 我 (Wǒ)
  • 我是学生。 — I am a student.
  • 他认识我。 — He knows me.

content.text: In Chinese, '你' means {{c1::you (singular)}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐ}}.
content.explanation: '你' (Nǐ) is the second-person singular pronoun. A respectful/formal version is '您' (Nín), used when addressing elders or superiors.
content.spanish: En chino, '你' significa 'Tú / Usted'. Pronunciación: Nǐ.
content.usage:
Term: 你 (Nǐ)
  • 你是谁? — Who are you?
  • 我爱你。 — I love you.

content.text: In spoken Chinese, the pronoun for He, She, and It all sound the same: {{c1::tā}}. The characters differ: He=他, She=她, It=它.
content.explanation: In written Chinese, '他' (he), '她' (she), and '它' (it) are distinct. But in spoken Mandarin, all three are pronounced identically as 'tā'. Context determines meaning.
content.spanish: En chino, 他/她/它 todos suenan igual: 'tā' (él/ella/ello). Los caracteres son distintos.
content.usage:
Term: 他/她/它 (tā)
  • 她是我的老师。 — She is my teacher.
  • 他很忙。 — He is very busy.

content.text: In Chinese, '我们' means {{c1::We / us}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǒmen}}.
content.explanation: '我们' (Wǒmen) is the first-person plural pronoun. The suffix '们' (men) is added to singular pronouns to make them plural: 你们 (you all), 他们 (they).
content.spanish: En chino, '我们' significa 'Nosotros'. Pronunciación: Wǒmen.
content.usage:
Term: 我们 (Wǒmen)
  • 我们是朋友。 — We are friends.
  • 我们一起去吧! — Let's go together!

content.text: In Chinese, '他们' means {{c1::They}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Tāmen}}.
content.explanation: '他们' (Tāmen) is the third-person plural. For an all-female group '她们' is used, though '他们' is gender-neutral in plural.
content.spanish: En chino, '他们' significa 'Ellos/Ellas'. Pronunciación: Tāmen.
content.usage:
Term: 他们 (Tāmen)
  • 他们在哪里? — Where are they?
  • 他们都是工程师。 — They are all engineers.

content.text: In Chinese, the verb 'to be' (for identity/classification) is {{c1::是 (shì)}}. Example: 我是老师 = {{c2::I am a teacher}}.
content.explanation: '是' (shì) is used to link a subject with a noun (identity). Unlike English 'be', it is NOT used with adjectives. For adjectives, Chinese omits the verb entirely: 她很美 = She is beautiful.
content.spanish: En chino, 'ser/estar' con sustantivos se dice '是 (shì)'. No se usa con adjetivos.
content.usage:
Term: 是 (shì)
  • 这是我的手机。 — This is my phone.
  • 他是墨西哥人。 — He is Mexican.

content.text: In Chinese, '不是' means {{c1::am not / is not / are not}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Bú shì}}.
content.explanation: Negation in Chinese is formed by placing '不' (bù/bú) before the verb. '不是' (bú shì) negates '是'. Note the tone change: 不 changes from 4th to 2nd tone before another 4th tone.
content.spanish: En chino, '不是 (bú shì)' significa 'no ser'. La negación se forma con '不' antes del verbo.
content.usage:
Term: 不是 (Bú shì)
  • 我不是你的老师。 — I am not your teacher.
  • 这不是我的书包。 — This is not my backpack.

content.text: To turn any statement into a yes/no question in Chinese, add {{c1::吗 (ma)}} at the end. Example: 你是学生吗? = {{c2::Are you a student?}}
content.explanation: '吗' (ma) is a question particle that converts statements to yes/no questions with no word order change. This makes Chinese question formation simpler than in English.
content.spanish: En chino, añadir '吗 (ma)' al final de una oración la convierte en pregunta de sí/no.
content.usage:
Term: 吗 (ma)
  • 你饿吗? — Are you hungry?
  • 他是你朋友吗? — Is he your friend?

content.text: In Chinese, the possessive particle is {{c1::的 (de)}}. Example: 我的书 = {{c2::My book}}.
content.explanation: '的' (de) works like the English apostrophe 's. It follows a pronoun or noun to show possession. 你的 = your, 他的 = his, 我们的 = our.
content.spanish: En chino, '的 (de)' funciona como el apóstrofe 's para indicar posesión. 我的 = mío, 你的 = tuyo.
content.usage:
Term: 的 (de)
  • 这是我的电脑。 — This is my computer.
  • 他们的办公室在三楼。 — Their office is on the third floor.

content.text: In Chinese, '你们' means {{c1::You all / You (plural)}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐmen}}.
content.explanation: '你们' is the informal second-person plural. The formal plural '您们' (Nínmen) exists but is rarely used — in formal settings, addressing a group, '各位' (gèwèi = everyone) is preferred.
content.spanish: En chino, '你们' significa 'Vosotros / Ustedes'. Pronunciación: Nǐmen.
content.usage:
Term: 你们 (Nǐmen)
  • 你们好! — Hello everyone! (group greeting)
  • 你们准备好了吗? — Are you all ready?

content.scenario: Chinese A1: There Is / There Are — 有 🧩
content.text: In Chinese, '有' (yǒu) expresses {{c1::existence / there is / to have}}. Example: 这里有一家咖啡店 = {{c2::There is a coffee shop here}}.
content.explanation: '有' (yǒu) serves as both 'to have' and 'there is/are'. Its negative is '没有' (méiyǒu) — does not have / there is not.
content.spanish: En chino, '有 (yǒu)' significa 'tener / haber'. Su negativo es '没有 (méiyǒu)'.
content.usage:
Term: 有 (yǒu)
  • 我有一个问题。 — I have a question.
  • 这里没有信号。 — There is no signal here.

content.text: In Chinese, '在' (zài) expresses {{c1::location / to be at / to be in}}. Example: 我在北京 = {{c2::I am in Beijing}}.
content.explanation: '在' (zài) is used instead of '是' when talking about location. It means 'to be at/in' a place. Note: 我是北京 is grammatically wrong for location.
content.spanish: En chino, '在 (zài)' indica ubicación: 'estar en un lugar'. Se usa en lugar de '是' para expresar dónde está algo.
content.usage:
Term: 在 (zài)
  • 她在图书馆。 — She is at the library.
  • 文件在桌子上。 — The file is on the desk.

content.text: In Chinese, '我叫...' means {{c1::My name is... / I am called...}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǒ jiào...}}
content.explanation: '叫' (jiào) literally means 'to be called'. '我叫Jesus' — I am called Jesus. You can also say '我的名字是Jesus' (Wǒ de míngzi shì Jesus), which uses '是'.
content.spanish: En chino, '我叫...' significa 'Me llamo...'. Pronunciación: Wǒ jiào.
content.usage:
Term: 我叫 (Wǒ jiào)
  • 大家好，我叫 Jesus。 — Hello everyone, my name is Jesus.

content.text: In Chinese, adjective sentences require the intensifier {{c1::很 (hěn)}} before the adjective. Example: 她很忙 = {{c2::She is very busy}}.
content.explanation: In Chinese, when an adjective is used as the predicate, '很' must be placed before it. While '很' literally means 'very', in simple statements it acts as a linking word and its intensity is often neutral.
content.spanish: En chino, '很 (hěn)' es necesario entre el sujeto y el adjetivo predicativo. Técnicamente significa 'muy'.
content.usage:
Term: 很 (hěn)
  • 他很高。 — He is tall.
  • 中文很难。 — Chinese is difficult.

content.text: In Chinese, '也' means {{c1::also / too / as well}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Yě}}.
content.explanation: '也' (yě) is placed after the subject and before the verb, not at the end of a sentence like in English. Position matters: 我也是 = I am also / Me too.
content.spanish: En chino, '也 (yě)' significa 'también'. Va después del sujeto y antes del verbo.
content.usage:
Term: 也 (yě)
  • A: 我是工程师。— B: 我也是。(Me too / I'm also [one].)
  • 她也学中文。 — She also studies Chinese.

content.text: In Chinese, '这' means {{c1::this}} (Zhè) and '那' means {{c2::that}} (Nà).
content.explanation: '这' (zhè) and '那' (nà) are demonstrative pronouns/adjectives. Add '个' (ge) for 'this one / that one': 这个 / 那个. They are used with '是' to identify things.
content.spanish: En chino, '这 (zhè)' = este/esta y '那 (nà)' = ese/aquel.
content.usage:
Term: 这 (zhè) / 那 (nà)
  • 这是什么? — What is this?
  • 那个人是谁? — Who is that person?

content.text: In Chinese, '谁' means {{c1::Who}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Shéi / Shuí}}.
content.explanation: '谁' (shéi or shuí, both pronunciations are correct) is the question word for 'who'. Chinese question words stay in the same position as the answer would be.
content.spanish: En chino, '谁 (shéi)' significa '¿Quién?'. Las palabras interrogativas mantienen su posición en la oración.
content.usage:
Term: 谁 (Shéi)
  • 你是谁? — Who are you?
  • 谁是你的老板? — Who is your boss?

content.text: In Chinese, '什么' means {{c1::What}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Shénme}}.
content.explanation: '什么' (shénme) is the most common question word. Like other Chinese question words, it stays in the same position as the answer: 你叫什么名字? (You called what name?)
content.spanish: En chino, '什么 (shénme)' significa '¿Qué?'. Permanece en la posición de la respuesta.
content.usage:
Term: 什么 (Shénme)
  • 这是什么? — What is this?
  • 你要什么? — What do you want?

content.scenario: Chinese A1: Numbers — One to Five 🧩
content.text: In Chinese, the numbers 1-5 are: 一(yī), 二(èr), 三(sān), 四(sì), 五(wǔ). What is {{c1::三 (sān)}} and {{c2::五 (wǔ)}} in English?
content.explanation: Chinese numerals are phonetically simple and consistent. 一=1, 二=2, 三=3, 四=4, 五=5. Numbers are essential for counting, phone numbers, prices, and telling time.
content.spanish: En chino: 一(yī)=1, 二(èr)=2, 三(sān)=3, 四(sì)=4, 五(wǔ)=5.
content.usage:
Term: 一二三四五 (yī èr sān sì wǔ)
  • 我要三个包子。 — I want three buns.
  • 我们有五个人。 — We have five people.

content.scenario: Chinese A1: Not Have — 没有 🧩
content.text: In Chinese, '没有' means {{c1::don't have / there is not}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Méiyǒu}}.
content.explanation: '没有' (méiyǒu) is the negation of '有' (to have). It is also used like 'there is not': 没有人 = There is nobody. In everyday speech, '没有' is often shortened to '没' (méi).
content.spanish: En chino, '没有 (méiyǒu)' significa 'no tener / no haber'. Es la negación de '有'.
content.usage:
Term: 没有 (Méiyǒu)
  • 我没有钱。 — I don't have money.
  • 没有问题! — No problem!
